questions.text: What is the defining characteristic of MST's Hybrid Layer-1 architecture?

options.text: A public blockchain that uses a sidechain for private transactions
A single network with multiple execution zones sharing one consensus engine
Two separate blockchains connected by a multi-signature bridge
A private ledger that periodically publishes hashes to Ethereum

questions.explanation: The hybrid architecture uses one consensus engine (Trika POSA) to finalise blocks for all zones simultaneously. Options A, C, and D all describe architectures with separate validator sets or external bridges - exactly what MST's design avoids.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

The hybrid architecture uses one consensus engine (Trika POSA) to finalise blocks for all zones simultaneously. Options A, C, and D all describe architectures with separate validator sets or external bridges - exactly what MST's design avoids.

questions.text: In MST's hybrid architecture, the gas token MSTC is used in:

options.text: Only the Public Zone
Only Permissioned Zones
Both the Public Zone and all Permissioned Zones
A separate ERC-20 token must be deployed for each zone

questions.explanation: MSTC is the universal gas token across every zone. Whether a user transacts in the Public Zone or a Permissioned Zone, gas fees are paid in MSTC. This unified economic model prevents token fragmentation.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C

MSTC is the universal gas token across every zone. Whether a user transacts in the Public Zone or a Permissioned Zone, gas fees are paid in MSTC. This unified economic model prevents token fragmentation.

questions.text: Which statement about the Public Zone is TRUE?

options.text: Anyone can deploy a smart contract, and all state is publicly visible
Only whitelisted developers can deploy contracts, but state is public
Anyone can deploy contracts, but state is encrypted and hidden
The Public Zone only processes token transfers, not smart contracts

questions.explanation: The Public Zone is fully permissionless: open validator set, open contract deployment, and completely transparent state. This is what enables censorship-resistant DeFi, NFTs, and dApps.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: A

The Public Zone is fully permissionless: open validator set, open contract deployment, and completely transparent state. This is what enables censorship-resistant DeFi, NFTs, and dApps.

questions.text: A Permissioned Zone on MST is characterised by:

options.text: Open validator access and public state
Whitelisted validators and confidential state
A completely separate blockchain with its own consensus
Free gas for all transactions

questions.explanation: Permissioned Zones restrict validator participation to identity-verified, whitelisted entities. State data is visible only to authorised parties, making these zones suitable for enterprise compliance, CBDCs, and confidential business logic.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

Permissioned Zones restrict validator participation to identity-verified, whitelisted entities. State data is visible only to authorised parties, making these zones suitable for enterprise compliance, CBDCs, and confidential business logic.

questions.text: What is the primary advantage of Inter-Zone Communication (IZC) over an external blockchain bridge?

options.text: IZC uses a dedicated set of relayers with higher security
IZC is validated by the same consensus validators, eliminating additional trust assumptions
IZC encrypts all messages end-to-end
IZC requires only one confirmation instead of multiple

questions.explanation: External bridges rely on separate validator sets or multisig committees - a major attack vector (Wormhole, Ronin hacks). MST's IZC is secured by the same validators that finalise blocks for both zones, so its security is equivalent to the consensus itself. No extra trust layer.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

External bridges rely on separate validator sets or multisig committees - a major attack vector (Wormhole, Ronin hacks). MST's IZC is secured by the same validators that finalise blocks for both zones, so its security is equivalent to the consensus itself. No extra trust layer.

questions.text: A supply-chain consortium wants to keep wholesale pricing confidential but let consumers verify product authenticity on-chain. Which MST configuration achieves this?

options.text: Deploy everything on the Public Zone with encryption
Pricing logic in a Permissioned Zone, authenticity verification in the Public Zone, linked via IZC
Run a completely separate private blockchain for the consortium
Store pricing off-chain and only put authenticity on the Public Zone

questions.explanation: This is the classic hybrid use case. Confidential data stays in the Permissioned Zone; public verification lives in the Public Zone. IZC links the two so a shipment event in the Permissioned Zone triggers a verifiable commitment in the Public Zone - consumers verify without seeing pricing.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

This is the classic hybrid use case. Confidential data stays in the Permissioned Zone; public verification lives in the Public Zone. IZC links the two so a shipment event in the Permissioned Zone triggers a verifiable commitment in the Public Zone - consumers verify without seeing pricing.

questions.text: A validator in MST's network can participate in:

options.text: Only one zone at a time
Both the Public Zone and any Permissioned Zone for which it meets the access criteria
Only the Public Zone - permissioned zones use separate validators
Only permissioned zones if they hold enough MSTC

questions.explanation: A validator can serve multiple zones simultaneously, provided it satisfies each zone's requirements. For the Public Zone, any staked validator can participate. For a Permissioned Zone, additional identity/KYC verification is required. This overlapping validator set is what makes IZC trustless.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

A validator can serve multiple zones simultaneously, provided it satisfies each zone's requirements. For the Public Zone, any staked validator can participate. For a Permissioned Zone, additional identity/KYC verification is required. This overlapping validator set is what makes IZC trustless.

questions.text: How does state partitioning work in MST's hybrid architecture?

options.text: All zones share one global Merkle tree
Each zone maintains its own state trie, with a single consensus block header committing to all zone roots
State is stored off-chain and only hashes are on-chain
Permissioned Zones have no state - they are purely for computation

questions.explanation: Each zone has its own independent Merkle Patricia Trie with its own root hash. The consensus block header contains a cryptographic commitment to all zone roots. A light client verifying a Public Zone transaction only needs the Public Zone root - Permissioned Zone data remains cryptographically committed but not revealed.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

Each zone has its own independent Merkle Patricia Trie with its own root hash. The consensus block header contains a cryptographic commitment to all zone roots. A light client verifying a Public Zone transaction only needs the Public Zone root - Permissioned Zone data remains cryptographically committed but not revealed.

questions.text: Why is a pure public blockchain (like Ethereum) unsuitable for a central bank issuing a CBDC?

options.text: Public chains are too slow for any financial application
The central bank cannot control the validator set or keep sensitive monetary operations confidential
Public chains cannot handle token transfers
Gas fees on public chains are always zero

questions.explanation: A central bank must control who can mint and burn currency (regulated entities only) and keep monetary policy operations confidential. On a pure public chain, any validator can process transactions and all state is visible - regulators lose control and confidentiality. MST's hybrid architecture solves this by placing minting/burning in a Permissioned Zone while letting retail CBDC circulate in the Public Zone.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

A central bank must control who can mint and burn currency (regulated entities only) and keep monetary policy operations confidential. On a pure public chain, any validator can process transactions and all state is visible - regulators lose control and confidentiality. MST's hybrid architecture solves this by placing minting/burning in a Permissioned Zone while letting retail CBDC circulate in the Public Zone.

questions.text: "In MST's hybrid architecture, a Permissioned Zone runs on a completely separate consensus engine from the Public Zone, and they communicate via an external bridge."
questions.explanation: 1 mark: Correctly states FALSE and explains that a single consensus engine secures all zones, not separate engines with an external bridge.
questions.modelAnswer:
📊 Marks Breakdown
